feed.title: JS | Node
feed.subtitle: Combined JS/Node.js runtime & tooling feed: Node.js, pnpm, jsDelivr (blog + status), Bun, Deno, NodeSource, Total.js, Vite, Next.js, Vue, Svelte, React, JavaScript Weekly, ReactLibs, Bootstrap, jQuery, V8, PWABuilder, npm status, npmx, and the OpenJS Foundation blog.

entry.title: j-Importer - Total.js
entry.content: The blog introduces j-Importer, a component from the jComponent library by Total.js, which allows importing HTML templates, scripts, or styles dynamically based on conditions. It explains how to set up and use j-Importer to import a pop-up form triggered by a button.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: Beekeeping - recording damage caused by a brown bear
entry.content: Watch how we built a dynamic, multi-step form using Total.js UI Builder and processed submissions via Flow - all without touching the source code.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: j-Box - Total.js UI component
entry.content: The j-Box component from the Total.js jComponent UI library is a versatile tool for creating customizable pop-up windows, such as forms or galleries. This blog demonstrates its setup, attributes, and integration with other components, using examples to show how to build pop-up forms with menus, content, and buttons.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.content: A quick overview of all changes and news from the entire Total.js Platform. Read more about our work.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.content: A quick overview of all changes and news from the entire Total.js Platform. Read more about our work.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: Revolutionize Your IoT Management with Total.js IoT Platform: Simplify, Monitor, and Optimize
entry.content: The Total.js IoT Platform simplifies the management of diverse IoT devices by unifying data, monitoring, and automation in one place. It helps you streamline your operations, visualize data in real-time, and focus on insights instead of technical complexity.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: How to install IoT platform — Total.js
entry.content: This blog shows how to install the Total.js IoT platform step by step, including setting up the database, downloading the code, and running the application. It’s the first part of a series that explains how to build the full platform by adding components like Stream, OpenReports, Flow, and OpenPlatform.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: July report 2025
entry.content: A quick overview of all changes and news from the entire Total.js Platform. Read more about our work.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: How to install stream to IoT platform — Total.js
entry.content: This blog continues the IoT platform setup by explaining how to install and run the Stream application, which collects incoming data from sensors. You’ll learn how to create a driver and device, secure the data with a token, and send real-time values to your platform using a simple API request.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: How to install OpenReports — IoT platform
entry.content: This blog continues the IoT platform installation series by guiding you through installing and setting up OpenReports, a flexible reporting tool from Total.js. You’ll learn how to connect it to your database, create views, and generate reports, enabling you to analyze and manage your IoT data more effectively.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: How to install Flow — IoT platform
entry.content: This blog shows you how to install and set up the Flow application as part of the Total.js IoT platform, enabling real-time data processing, alerts, and automation through a low-code visual interface. By the end, you’ll have IoT platform, Stream, OpenReports, and Flow all running together with a basic architecture ready for further use.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: Total.js Tables is here!
entry.content: Total.js Tables is a no-code tool for designing and managing database tables on top of PostgreSQL.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: How to install OpenPlatform — IoT platform
entry.content: This blog explains how to install and configure OpenPlatform as the final step in setting up the Total.js IoT platform, allowing you to integrate IoT Platform, OpenReports, and Flow into one unified system. With OpenPlatform, you can manage users, permissions, and apps seamlessly, making your IoT data handling more efficient and secure.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: IoT platform — Total.js
entry.content: The final blog in the IoT Platform series demonstrates how to fully utilize the platform by working with drivers, devices, sensors, data ingestion, visualizations, and integrations. It also explores advanced features such as dataviews, zones, maps, reports, alerts, simulations, and panels to showcase the platform’s complete capabilities.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: August report 2025
entry.content: A quick overview of all changes and news from the entire Total.js Platform. Read more about our work.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: How to create Google Gemini AI component in Total.js Flow
entry.content: This blog shows how to create a custom Google Gemini AI component for Total.js Flow, enabling you to integrate AI-powered text generation directly into your workflows. It covers everything from setting up the component to configuring its API key, models, and output for real-time AI responses.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: September report 2025
entry.content: A quick overview of all changes and news from the entire Total.js Platform. Read more about our work.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: IoT Platform Update: New Features and Enhancements
entry.content: The updated Total.js IoT Platform lets you create reports and publish devices, zones, or sensors as API endpoints, with a cleaner design and enhanced data access.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: New universal drivers for IoT Platform
entry.content: Meet the new universal drivers for the Total.js IoT Platform — built for flexibility and easy customization. From electrometers to switches, these ready-to-use drivers simplify IoT data processing and integration.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: Total.js without NPM? Absolutely. Run the framework from pure Node.js
entry.content: Run Total.js without NPM - using pure Node.js, dynamic loading and zero local framework dependencies.
entry.dc:creator: Total.js Blog

entry.title: pnpm 11.11-11.14
entry.content: pnpm 11.11 through 11.14 add native workspace release management (pnpm change, pnpm lane, and a bare pnpm version -r), a pnpm doctor command that diagnoses your installation end to end, the pnpm access and pnpm team commands for managing packages and organizations on the registry, convergence overrides, and scheme-carrying peerDependencies specifiers. They also fix a path-traversal vulnerability, cut peak memory during cold-cache resolution by roughly 30%, and resolve a peer dependency deadlock.
entry.dc:creator: pnpm Blog

entry.title: npm 12, TypeScript 7, and Bun in Rust
entry.content: #​794 — July 14, 2026 Read on the Web JavaScript Weekly 📈 TanStack's npm Package Download Charts — A quick way to chart package popularity over time, look for trends, and see what libraries and tools have soared or fallen. The default view compares TanStack's packages against several common packages, but you can enter any packages you wish, or see pre-defined sets like build tools, component libraries, or UI frameworks. TanStack Your AI Writes the Code. CodeRabbit Reviews It — AI can write the c
entry.dc:creator: JavaScript Weekly

entry.title: Next.js Security Release and Our Next Patch Release
entry.content: Next.js is moving to a formal security release process
entry.dc:creator: Next.js Blog

entry.title: Meet the Node.js Interactive Speakers at RenderATL 2026
entry.content: Node.js Interactive comes to RenderATL with talks on AI-assisted development, performance, open source infrastructure, documentation, and the engineering practices teams need now.
entry.dc:creator: OpenJS Foundation

entry.title: Rewriting Bun in Rust
entry.content: Why & how we rewrote Bun from Zig to Rust
entry.dc:creator: Bun

entry.title: Node.js 26.5.0 (Current)
entry.content: Node.js 26.5.0 (Current)
entry.dc:creator: Node.js Blog

entry.title: OpenJS Foundation Security Update: Q2 2026
entry.content: Q2 2026 brought a Node.js 26 release, a major security release addressing 18 vulnerabilities with a streamlined 36-to-7-step process, the removal of the security embargo requirement, and a new LLM-assisted report classifier and web viewer for release management.
entry.dc:creator: OpenJS Foundation

entry.title: What's new in ECMAScript 2026 (and usable today)
entry.content: #​793 — July 7, 2026 Read on the Web JavaScript Weekly Vite+ Beta: A Web Dev Toolchain Behind One Command — Vite+ is the Vite team’s ‘unified toolchain’ that brings Vite, Vitest, Oxlint, and similar tools together under a single vp command, whether for running a dev server, tests, formatting, or bundling. VoidZero 💡 Vite+ was originally intended to be a commercial project to fund work on Vite and related projects, but was open sourced under the MIT license earlier this year. Still Writing Tests
entry.dc:creator: JavaScript Weekly

entry.title: pnpm 11.10
entry.content: pnpm 11.10 adds the _auth setting for CI-friendly registry authentication, new pnpm prefix and pnpm issues commands, and the ability for pnpm self-update to install pnpm v12 (the Rust port). It also improves pnpm up accuracy, speeds up resolution against registries that ignore abbreviated metadata, and hardens global package management, pnpm deploy, and pnpm pack-app.
entry.dc:creator: pnpm Blog

entry.title: npmx rainbow comet
entry.content: npmx 0.16 is out! This period was especially full of good news. And it’s particularly great that, amid the constant search for vulnerabilities and risks, we’ve started celebrating more and taking joy in what we do - and what our favorite maintainers and authors do.
entry.dc:creator: npmx

entry.title: What’s new in Svelte: July 2026
entry.content: This month brought a real shift in how SvelteKit projects are configured. You can now define your SvelteKit config directly inside vite.config.js and skip svelte.config.js entirely. We also got the first preview of explicit environment variables, which will eventually replace $env/* modules in SvelteKit 3. On top of that, the language tools and the sv CLI both caught up with Svelte's new {const ...} declaration tags, so the whole toolchain is now in sync. Let's dive in! What's new in SvelteKit Y
entry.dc:creator: Svelte Blog

entry.title: Will npm v12 reject your .npmrc?
entry.content: #​792 — June 30, 2026 Read on the Web JavaScript Weekly Deno 2.9 Released — A huge release for the runtime: deno desktop turns scripts and framework-based projects into native, self-contained desktop apps, quick migration from npm/pnpm/Yarn/Bun lockfiles, big test runner improvements, ~2x faster cold starts, and a target of Node.js 26 compatibility. Bartek Iwańczuk 44 Recorded PostgreSQL Talks Are Now Available to Watch — Explore 44 talks about PostgreSQL-backed app development, Postgres perform
entry.dc:creator: JavaScript Weekly

entry.title: Turbopack: What's New in Next.js 16.3
entry.content: Turbopack in Next.js 16.3 adds development memory eviction, persistent file cache for builds, experimental Rust React Compiler, and `import.meta.glob` support.
entry.dc:creator: Next.js Blog

entry.title: Next.js 16.3: AI Improvements
entry.content: Actionable errors with paste-ready fix prompts, first-party Skills for the dev loop and Cache Components, and tooling that keeps coding agents in sync with your project.
entry.dc:creator: Next.js Blog

entry.title: SQLRooms Joins the OpenJS Foundation Under the Open Visualization Working Group
entry.content: The OpenJS Foundation is excited to welcome SQLRooms as a new project within the Open Visualization Working Group as part of the vis.gl open source project, expanding the growing ecosystem of open source tools that help developers explore, analyze, and visualize data on the web.
entry.dc:creator: OpenJS Foundation

entry.title: Debug a Node.js Memory Leak in Minutes with AI-Powered Heap Snapshot Analysis
entry.content: Learn how to detect, analyze, and fix a Node.js memory leak using AI-powered Heap Snapshot analysis directly inside VS Code, Cursor, Windsurf and Antigravity.
entry.dc:creator: NodeSource Blog

entry.title: Next.js 16.3: Instant Navigations
entry.content: A new suite of tools that brings the responsiveness of a single-page app to the server-centric model of Next.js.
entry.dc:creator: Next.js Blog

entry.title: Deno 2.9
entry.content: `deno desktop` for building native desktop apps from web tech, first-class migration from npm/pnpm/yarn/Bun, CSS module imports, snapshot and parameterized testing, smaller `deno compile --bundle` binaries, and Node.js 26 compatibility.
entry.dc:creator: Deno

entry.title: cosmos.gl v3 Is Here
entry.content: Today, the team is shipping v3 – the most significant release in the project's history.
entry.dc:creator: OpenJS Foundation

entry.content: Node.js 26.4.0 (Current)

entry.title: Node.js 22.23.1 (LTS)
entry.content: Node.js 22.23.1 (LTS)
entry.dc:creator: Node.js Blog

entry.title: Babel 8.0, Vite 8.1, and TypeScript 7.0 RC
entry.content: #​791 — June 23, 2026 Read on the Web JavaScript Weekly Desktop Apps With deno desktop — Deno 2.9 (or the 'canary' build now ) can turn JavaScript projects into self-contained apps on macOS, Windows, and Linux. Unlike Electron, you can opt to use the default OS WebView or a bundled Chromium backend, plus you get cross-compilation and automatic support for apps built on frameworks like Next.js and SvelteKit. The Deno Project 📉 It's not in the post, but a --compress option gets packaged app sizes
entry.dc:creator: JavaScript Weekly

entry.title: Vite 8.1 is out!
entry.content: Vite 8.1 is out! ​ June 23, 2026 Vite 8 was released in March with a single unified bundler powered by Rolldown , opening the door to further improvements. It is now seeing 41.6 million weekly downloads, almost reaching the total downloads of Vite 7. Alongside resolving upgrade regressions, we've been working on new features, and we're excited to announce the release of Vite 8.1. Quick links: Docs Translations: 简体中文 , 日本語 , Español , Português , 한국어 , Deutsch , فارسی GitHub Changelog Play online
entry.dc:creator: Vite Blog

entry.title: pnpm 11.9
entry.content: pnpm 11.9 computes missing tarball integrity for registries that cannot publish checksums, adds pnpm sbom --exclude-peers, improves audit performance on cyclic lockfiles, fixes peer-resolution nondeterminism, and tightens exclusion handling for minimumReleaseAge and trustPolicy.
entry.dc:creator: pnpm Blog

entry.title: Blocking Install Scripts Is Not a Silver Bullet
entry.content: npm v12 blocks install scripts by default, but supply chain attacks won't disappear. Learn why runtime execution, the Node.js permission model, and sandboxing still matter.
entry.dc:creator: NodeSource Blog

entry.title: performative-ui: Components That Signal How Oversubscribed Your Round Is
entry.content: performative-ui is a satirical-but-real React component library of cliche AI-startup landing-page tropes. It ships typed, working components like ASCII heroes, node-graph backgrounds, and a Claude Code style wibbling spinner.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Fuse.js: Fuzzy Search That Forgives Your Typos
entry.content: Fuse.js is a tiny, zero-dependency fuzzy-search library that adds typo-tolerant search to any app without a backend. Version 7.4 brings multi-word token search and parallel search across Web Workers.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: get-value: Reaching Into Nested Objects Without Faceplanting
entry.content: get-value is a tiny zero-dependency utility for reading nested object values with dot-notation paths, and it is the rare one that handles keys with dots in them. Version 4.1 adds Map-like object support on top of a TypeScript rewrite.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Heerich.js: Stacking Voxels Into Crisp SVG, No WebGL Required
entry.content: Heerich.js is a tiny zero-dependency engine that builds 3D voxel scenes and renders them to clean SVG vectors. No canvas, no WebGL, just stackable blocks, boolean carving, and four camera projections.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Hiraki: Slide-In Drawers Without the Dependency Hangover
entry.content: Hiraki is a zero-dependency, headless React drawer primitive with velocity-aware gestures and snap points in all four directions. This early-stage library chases Vaul-class polish in about 10 KB without pulling in Radix or Framer Motion.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: html-react-parser: Turn HTML Strings Into Real React Elements
entry.content: html-react-parser converts raw HTML strings into genuine React elements you can intercept, replace, and restyle. Learn how to render CMS content without reaching for dangerouslySetInnerHTML.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Ink: When Your Terminal Learns to Speak React
entry.content: Ink is a React renderer that turns components, hooks, and flexbox into interactive command-line interfaces. Learn how to build polished terminal apps with the same mental model you already use on the web.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Ky: Fetch With the Sharp Edges Sanded Off
entry.content: Ky is a tiny, zero-dependency HTTP client built on top of the Fetch API, adding retries, timeouts, hooks, and JSON ergonomics in about 4 KB. Here is how it turns raw fetch boilerplate into clean, composable requests.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: json-render Ink: When the LLM Paints Your Terminal
entry.content: json-render Ink turns guardrailed JSON specs into interactive terminal UIs built on Ink, so an AI can compose a CLI you can actually trust. Define a component catalog, let the model emit constrained JSON, and stream it straight to the console.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: nanotags: Reactive Web Components That Skip the Shadow DOM Drama
entry.content: nanotags is a sub-3KB wrapper around native Custom Elements that adds nanostores reactivity, a fully typed builder, props, refs, and automatic cleanup. Here is how it makes light-DOM, hydration-first components pleasant to write.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Nitro Fetch: The Drop-In Fetch That Leaves React Native's Built-In One in the Dust
entry.content: Nitro Fetch is a native-powered, Web-standard drop-in replacement for React Native's fetch built on Margelo's Nitro Modules. It adds HTTP/3, prefetching, streaming, and off-thread JSON parsing while keeping the same API you already know.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Nitro Player: A Batteries-Included Music Engine for React Native
entry.content: Nitro Player is a full-featured React Native audio engine built on Nitro Modules, shipping playlists, a 5-band equalizer, offline downloads, and native Android Auto and CarPlay support. This guide walks through the queue model, hooks, and equalizer that make it a modern alternative for music apps.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: React-Calendar: A Calendar That Skips the Moment.js Diet
entry.content: React-Calendar is a flexible, accessible, dependency-light date and date-range picker component for React. Drop it in, restyle it, and pick days, months, years, or whole decades without dragging moment.js into your bundle.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: React Color: The Variety Pack of Color Pickers Your App Has Been Missing
entry.content: @uiw/react-color is a modular, TypeScript-first collection of React color pickers, from Photoshop-style panels to curated swatch grids. Here is how to drop one in, compose your own, and theme it for dark mode.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Create React Native Library: Skip the Boilerplate, Ship the Module
entry.content: Create React Native Library is Callstack's CLI for scaffolding publishable React Native libraries, complete with new-architecture native modules, example apps, and build tooling. Here is how to go from an empty folder to a working Turbo Module, Fabric view, or Nitro module in minutes.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Scroll Without End: Effortless Pagination with react-infinite-scroll-component
entry.content: react-infinite-scroll-component triggers a callback when readers approach the end of a list, so you can fetch and append the next page on demand. The v7 rewrite brings IntersectionObserver, zero dependencies, a tiny bundle, and a fresh hook API.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Shaders: GPU Eye-Candy Without Writing a Single Line of WGSL
entry.content: Shaders is a component system for creative WebGPU effects in the browser, with wrappers for React, Vue, Svelte, Solid and vanilla JS. Drop in over a hundred GPU-powered gradients, blurs, distortions and post-effects as declarative components.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Shimmer From Structure: Skeletons That Measure Themselves
entry.content: shimmer-from-structure generates loading skeletons by measuring your real components at runtime, so you never hand-build a duplicate skeleton again. This guide covers static cards, dynamic templateProps, Suspense fallbacks, and global theming across React, Vue, Svelte, Angular, and SolidJS.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: SSGOI: Give Your Web Pages the Transitions a Native App Would Brag About
entry.content: SSGOI turns plain route swaps into fluid, spring-physics page transitions that work in every browser and with any router. Here is how it works and how to wire it into a React or Next.js app.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: STRICH: Production-Grade Barcode Scanning Without Leaving the Browser
entry.content: STRICH is a commercial JavaScript SDK that turns a device camera into a fast, reliable barcode and QR scanner running entirely in the browser. Here is how it works, how to wire it into React, and how it stacks up against the free alternatives.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: SVGInject: Teaching Your Images to Speak CSS
entry.content: SVGInject swaps plain img tags for inline svg elements at runtime, so you can finally style every path, gradient, and group with plain CSS. No build step, no framework lock-in, and around 3.5 KB gzipped.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: react-inlinesvg: Give Your SVGs CSS Superpowers
entry.content: react-inlinesvg loads SVGs from URLs, paths, or raw strings and injects them inline into the DOM as real elements. That means you can style, recolor, and animate them with plain CSS, just like any other markup.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Tabulator: Interactive Data Grids Without the Dependency Tax
entry.content: Tabulator turns any HTML table, JavaScript array, or JSON feed into a fully interactive data grid with sorting, filtering, inline editing, and export built in. It ships zero dependencies, an MIT license, and works with React, Vue, Angular, or plain JavaScript.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: React MD Editor: The Textarea That Thinks It's an Editor
entry.content: A lightweight, textarea-based markdown editor for React with live split-pane preview, dark mode, and a fully customizable toolbar. Drop it in with two props and start writing.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: relative-time-element: The HTML Tag That Says 5 Minutes Ago
entry.content: A look at GitHub's tiny Web Component for formatting timestamps as localized, auto-updating relative text. Learn how to render dates that adapt to every visitor's locale and timezone without shipping a date library.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: True Sheet: The Bottom Sheet That Lets the OS Do the Work
entry.content: True Sheet is a React Native library that renders genuine native bottom sheets on iOS and Android instead of reimplementing them in JavaScript. It delivers native gestures, blur, keyboard handling, and accessibility through a tidy imperative and declarative API.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Uniwind: Tailwind for React Native Without the Runtime Tax
entry.content: Uniwind brings Tailwind className styling to React Native and computes the styles at build time for serious performance. From the team behind Unistyles, it supports Tailwind v4, theming, and every native component out of the box.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: visx: Airbnb's Low-Level Lego Bricks for React Charts
entry.content: visx is Airbnb's collection of low-level visualization primitives that pair D3's math with React's rendering. Here is how to assemble your own custom charts without ever touching a D3 selection.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: web-audio-api: The Browser's Audio Engine, Now Running in Node
entry.content: A pure-JavaScript implementation of the W3C Web Audio API that runs on Node and Bun with zero native dependencies. Write audio code once and run it in the terminal, in CI, or on the server exactly as you would in a browser.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: zod-compiler: Zod at the Speed of Light, Without Touching Your Code
entry.content: zod-compiler is a bundler plugin that rewrites your Zod schemas into flat, inlined validation functions at build time. You keep the exact same Zod code and get up to 75x faster validation for free.
entry.dc:creator: ReactLibs

entry.title: Thursday, June 18, 2026 Security Releases
entry.content: Thursday, June 18, 2026 Security Releases
entry.dc:creator: Node.js Blog

entry.title: pnpm 11.8
entry.content: pnpm 11.8 adds install dry-run previews, Node.js package map generation, richer SBOM output, pnpm view defaulting to the current package, and correct pnpm run --no-bail exit codes. It also includes a config-dependency lockfile traversal fix and many install/update determinism fixes.
entry.dc:creator: pnpm Blog

entry.title: pnpm 11.7
entry.content: pnpm 11.7 adds a frozenStore setting for installing against a read-only package store, a --batch flag for publishing a whole workspace in one request, scope-specific auth tokens, and full resolving installs delegated to pacquet. It also hardens lockfile alias handling, makes several install paths deterministic, and ships a number of publish and Windows fixes.
entry.dc:creator: pnpm Blog

entry.title: intermittent Publish Failures
entry.content: Jun 12 , 12:57 UTC Resolved - This incident has been resolved. Jun 12 , 12:44 UTC Monitoring - A fix has been implemented and we are monitoring the results. Jun 12 , 12:33 UTC Identified - The issue has been identified and a fix is being implemented. Jun 12 , 12:08 UTC Investigating - We are currently investigating this issue.
entry.dc:creator: npm Status

entry.title: How We Designed a Node.js Production Debugging Experience with AI
entry.content: Learn how the NodeSource team designed a Node.js production debugging experience for modern IDEs using AI tools, rapid iteration, and continuous developer feedback.
entry.dc:creator: NodeSource Blog

entry.title: pnpm 11.6
entry.content: pnpm 11.6 adds a file-free way to supply registry authentication through npmconfig//… and pnpmconfig//… environment variables, raises the default network concurrency, and skips full re-resolution when only pnpm-lock.yaml is missing. It also infers platform fields for optional dependencies so foreign-platform binaries are never downloaded.
entry.dc:creator: pnpm Blog

entry.title: Why pnpm no longer expands environment variables in a repository's .npmrc
entry.content: pnpm used to expand $ placeholders everywhere it found them — including in the .npmrc and pnpm-workspace.yaml files that live inside the repository you just cloned. That turned out to be a way for a malicious repository to steal the secrets in your environment. As of v10.34.2 and v11.5.3, pnpm stops expanding environment variables in repository-controlled registry and credential settings.
entry.dc:creator: pnpm Blog

entry.title: Security at Scale: How NodeSource Remediated 21 Vulnerabilities Across Enterprise Node.js Environments

entry.dc:creator: Vite Blog

entry.content: This month we got a bunch of improvements in SvelteKit's forms and remote functions. Plus, a new query function ( .live(...) ) that makes accessing real-time data from the server easier. Keep an eye out for a few breaking changes in remote functions, if you're using those. Otherwise, enjoy all the new SvelteKit features and bug fixes in the latest versions of Svelte. Let's dive in! What's new in SvelteKit Form submit now returns a boolean to indicate submission validity for enhanced remote forms
entry.dc:creator: Svelte Blog

entry.title: pnpm 11.5
entry.content: pnpm 11.5 adds a hoistingLimits setting for controlling how far dependencies hoist in nodeLinker: hoisted installs, replaces the interactive prompt library to fix scrolling in long choice lists, recognizes staged publishes in the trust scale, and ships several install and dist-tag fixes.
entry.dc:creator: pnpm Blog

entry.title: pnpm 11.4
entry.content: pnpm 11.4 closes a cluster of supply-chain holes around lockfile integrity, credential scoping, git resolutions, patch files, and dependency aliases, makes tarball-integrity mismatches a hard install failure by default (with a narrowly-scoped --update-checksums opt-in), and changes pnpm runtime set to write to devEngines.runtime instead of engines.runtime by default.
entry.dc:creator: pnpm Blog

entry.title: TuxCare Joins OpenJS Foundation's Ecosystem Sustainability Program (ESP) to Provide Endless Security Support for End-of-Life JavaScript Projects
entry.content: TuxCare has joined the OpenJS Foundation as a Gold member and a strategic partner in our Ecosystem Sustainability Program (ESP)
entry.dc:creator: OpenJS Foundation

entry.title: pnpm 11.3
entry.content: pnpm 11.3 adds support for npm's staged publishing (pnpm stage), the new trustLockfile setting for skipping the supply-chain verification pass on already-trusted lockfiles, and native implementations of pnpm pkg, pnpm repo, and pnpm set-script. It also adds a --skip-manifest-obfuscation flag for pack / publish and cuts the memory footprint of minimumReleaseAge / trustPolicy verification on large workspaces.
entry.dc:creator: pnpm Blog

entry.title: The History of Date in JavaScript
entry.content: Discover the 31-year journey from JavaScript’s flawed `Date` API to Temporal in ECMAScript 2026 and Node.js 26. Learn how Temporal fixes time zones, parsing, immutability, and modern date handling in JavaScript.
entry.dc:creator: NodeSource Blog

entry.title: Vite 8.0 is out!
entry.content: Vite 8.0 is out! ​ March 12, 2026 We're thrilled to announce the stable release of Vite 8! When Vite first launched, we made a pragmatic bet on two bundlers: esbuild for speed during development, and Rollup for optimized production builds. That bet served us well for years. We're very grateful to the Rollup and esbuild maintainers. Vite wouldn't have succeeded without them. Today, it resolves into one: Vite 8 ships with Rolldown as its single, unified, Rust-based bundler, delivering up to 10-30x
entry.dc:creator: Vite Blog

entry.dc:creator: Bun

Here’s what you need to know: Upgrade now If you’re using any of these packages, upgrade them to their corresponding non-vulnerable versions: devalue : 5.6.2 svelte : 5.46.4 @sveltejs/kit : 2.49.5 @sveltejs/adapter-node : 5.5.1 For cross-dependent packages — svelte and @sveltejs/kit depend on devalue — patched versions already include upgraded dependencies. entry.dc:creator: Svelte Blog

entry.dc:creator: Bootstrap Blog

entry.title: How we made JSON.stringify more than twice as fast
entry.content: JSON.stringify is a core JavaScript function for serializing data. Its performance directly affects common operations across the web, from serializing data for a network request to saving data to localStorage . A faster JSON.stringify translates to quicker page interactions and more responsive applications. That’s why we’re excited to share that a recent engineering effort has made JSON.stringify in V8 more than twice as fast . This post breaks down the technical optimizations that made this imp
entry.dc:creator: V8

entry.title: Speculative Optimizations for WebAssembly using Deopts and Inlining
entry.content: In this blog post, we explain two optimizations for WebAssembly that we recently implemented in V8 and that shipped with Google Chrome M137, namely speculative call_indirect inlining and deoptimization support for WebAssembly. In combination, they allow us to generate better machine code by making assumptions based on runtime feedback. This speeds up WebAssembly execution, in particular for WasmGC programs. On a set of Dart microbenchmarks for example, the speedup by the combination of both opti
entry.dc:creator: V8

entry.dc:creator: Vite Blog

Serve files with `Bun.serve` through `routes`, `install.linkWorkspacePackages` option in `bunfig.toml`, `bun outdated` support for catalogs, `Bun.hash.rapidhash`, `node:net` compatibility, `vm.SyntheticModule` support, `HTTPParser` binding, and memory leak fixes.

entry.title: What’s new in Svelte: June 2025
entry.content: In addition to advancing the work on Async Svelte, the maintainers have been hard at work introducing long requested features like Attachments. This month, we'll share a bit about the new API along with a huge showcase of apps/sites built with Svelte... Let's dive in! What's new in Svelte and SvelteKit Attachments are essentially a more flexible and modern version of actions. Read more about their use-cases and improvements over actions in the PR ( #1500 ) or in the Svelte docs ( svelte@5.29.0 ,
entry.dc:creator: Svelte Blog

entry.title: Bun v1.2.15
entry.content: Fixes 11 issues (addressing 261 👍). bun audit scans dependencies for security vulnerabilities, bun pm view shows package metadata from npm. bun init adds a Cursor rule to use Bun instead of Node.js/Vite/npm/pnpm, `node:vm SourceTextModule` and `node:perf_hooks createHistogram` are now implemented.
entry.dc:creator: Bun

entry.title: A brief history of JavaScript
entry.content: In 30 years, JavaScript went from being a little scripting language to one of the world's most popular. Here are key moments to show how it has evolved and where it is headed.
entry.dc:creator: Deno

entry.title: Bun v1.2.14
entry.content: Fixes 39 issues (addressing 179 👍). Adds support for catalogs in bun install, --react flag for bun init, improved HTTP routing with method-specific routes, better TypeScript default module settings, fixes a HTTPS proxy issue impacting Codex, and several Node.js compatibility improvements including worker_threads reliability enhancements and http2 server improvements.
entry.dc:creator: Bun

entry.title: Bringing Svelte Summit to the whole community
entry.content: This month's Svelte Summit in Barcelona was a glorious celebration of the community. We had lively conversations, delightful dinners, and funny, imaginative and thought-provoking talks. For everyone who couldn't make it there in person, the conference also sold virtual tickets to the livestream, which give on-demand access to the talks. These ticket sales make it possible to (for example) hire AV technicians and ensure that the stream is reliable and high-quality. Running a conference is extreme
entry.dc:creator: Svelte Blog

entry.title: Bun v1.2.13
entry.content: Fixes 17 bugs (addressing 28 👍, adding +28 passing Node.js tests). Improved --hot stability and Node.js compatibility. Better `node:worker_threads` support, environment data, reduced memory usage for child_process IPC and worker_threads. Fixes BOM handling in `.npmrc` files. Fixes hot reloading on older versions of chrome

entry.dc:creator: Bootstrap Blog

entry.title: Bootstrap 5.3.6
entry.content: Bootstrap v5.3.6 was just released to migrate our documentation to Astro from Hugo. Also included are a few bug fixes and documentation updates. Here are some highlights: Ported the docs from Hugo to Astro for our own sanity! entry.dc:creator: Bootstrap Blog
